basmala: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate.
intro: Al-Zukhruf — “The Gold Ornaments” — takes its name from the gold (zukhruf) of this world, which God declares He would have lavished even upon the houses of the deniers: “Were it not that mankind would become a single community of disbelief, We would have made for those who deny the Compassionate roofs of silver.” Qushayrī reads here the sūra's great secret: the world has no worth in God's sight; were He to pour the whole of this world upon a man, it would not make up for his deprivation, for the entire world is of no value with Him. The sūra unfolds the Arabic Qurʾān inscribed in the Mother of the Book, the signs of creation — the earth-cradle, the reviving water, the pairs and the mounts —, Abraham's disavowal of the idols, Pharaoh's vanity glorying in his kingdom and demanding bracelets of gold, Jesus the servant offered as an example, and the deceptive gleam of the affluent who measure greatness by fortune. It culminates on the great reversal of the Day: “close friends will become enemies one to another on that Day, save the godfearing” — for, says Qushayrī, what was not for God ends in ruin, and only friendship forged in God, pure of all worldly interest, intercedes and endures. To the serene servants, the Garden and the vision of the Face; to the deniers, the gold that weighed nothing. The sūra seals itself on peace: “turn away from them and say: Peace!”
themes: the worthlessness of the world and its gold ornaments
the Arabic Qurʾān and the Mother of the Book
the vanity of Pharaoh and his gold
true friendship in God on the Day of reversal

versets.ar: حم
versets.tr: ḥā mīm
versets.trad: Ḥā. Mīm.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reads a secret of the Names in these two letters: the Ḥāʾ points to His Life (ḥayāt) and the Mīm to His Majesty (majd). And he hears in them an oath: “By My Life and My Majesty! What I have announced concerning My mercy toward My believing servants is pure truth and certainty.” The isolated letter opens the sūra on the mystery of the Name and the radiance of the Word.

mots.def: opening letters; per Qushayrī, Ḥāʾ for Life, Mīm for Majesty.

versets.ar: وَالْكِتَابِ الْمُبِينِ
versets.tr: wa-l-kitābi l-mubīni
versets.trad: By the manifest Book!
versets.commentaire: The oath continues: by this clear Qurʾān. Qushayrī links this verse to the preceding: God swears by His Life, His Majesty, and by His manifest Word that His promise of mercy to the believers is true. The Book is called “manifest” (mubīn) because it makes plain what it contains and discloses the way to whoever ponders it.

mots.def: mubīn: clear, that which makes plain.

versets.ar: إِنَّا جَعَلْنَاهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ
versets.tr: innā jaʿalnāhu qurʾānan ʿarabiyyan laʿallakum taʿqilūna
versets.trad: Truly We have made it an Arabic Qurʾān, that you might understand.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: We made it a Qurʾān in the Arabic tongue so that grasping its meaning would be made easy for you. Language is no contingent shell: it is the very condition of understanding. God accommodates His Word to the measure of His servants, leading them to the hidden sense by way of the manifest sense.

mots.racine: ق ر أ / ع ر ب

mots.def: ʿaqala: to grasp with the intellect.

versets.ar: وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتَابِ لَدَيْنَا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ
versets.tr: wa-innahu fī ummi l-kitābi ladaynā la-ʿaliyyun ḥakīmun
versets.trad: And it abides with Us in the Mother of the Book, sublime and full of wisdom.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “in the Mother of the Book with Us” means it is inscribed upon the Preserved Tablet (al-lawḥ al-maḥfūẓ). And: “sublime, wise”: lofty in rank, wise in its very description, subject to neither alteration nor change. The Qurʾān you recite has its eternal root with God, immutable, beyond the reach of all corruption.

mots.racine: أ م م / ك ت ب
mots.def: the Preserved Tablet, archetype of revelation.

mots.racine: ع ل و / ح ك م
mots.def: ʿaliyy: lofty; ḥakīm: wise, full of wisdom.

versets.ar: أَفَنَضْرِبُ عَنكُمُ الذِّكْرَ صَفْحًا أَن كُنتُمْ قَوْمًا مُّسْرِفِينَ
versets.tr: afa-naḍribu ʿankumu dh-dhikra ṣafḥan an kuntum qawman musrifīna
versets.trad: Shall We then withhold the Reminder from you because you are a people who exceed all bounds?
versets.commentaire: The question is a denial: no, We shall not do that. Qushayrī draws a subtle allusion: God does not withhold His Word or His address from one who persists in disobedience and exceeds in his conduct. How much less, then, will He withhold His subtle indulgences from one who has not failed in his faith — though stained with sins — so long as no breach has cracked his gnosis. Thus hope extends even to the sinning believer.

mots.racine: ض ر ب / ذ ك ر
mots.sens: shall We withhold from you the Reminder
mots.def: ḍaraba ʿan: to turn away, withhold; dhikr: the Reminder, the Qurʾān.
musrif: one who exceeds, given to excess.

versets.ar: وَكَمْ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن نَّبِيٍّ فِي الْأَوَّلِينَ
versets.tr: wa-kam arsalnā min nabiyyin fī l-awwalīna
versets.trad: How many a prophet did We send among the ancients!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī joins this verse to the next: no messenger came to them whom they met with affirmation; most, rather, denied him and rejected him, persisting in their error. The sending of prophets is a renewed mercy in every generation; the refusal, alas, repeats itself like a fatality of the blind soul.

versets.ar: وَمَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن نَّبِيٍّ إِلَّا كَانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِئُونَ
versets.tr: wa-mā yaʾtīhim min nabiyyin illā kānū bihi yastahziʾūna
versets.trad: Yet no prophet came to them but they mocked him.
versets.commentaire: Mockery is the constant weapon of denial. Qushayrī notes that no messenger reached them whom they received with faith: they were a multitude in denying and resisting him. Mockery masks the soul's inability to surrender; it belittles what it cannot reach, imagining it is thereby freed of it.

versets.ar: فَأَهْلَكْنَا أَشَدَّ مِنْهُم بَطْشًا وَمَضَىٰ مَثَلُ الْأَوَّلِينَ
versets.tr: fa-ahlaknā ashadda minhum baṭshan wa-maḍā mathalu l-awwalīna
versets.trad: So We destroyed men mightier than these in force, and the example of the ancients has gone before.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: not one of them escaped Us, We left none of them, and We took vengeance on those who did evil. The might of the destroyers availed nothing when the divine decree came. The example of the ancients “has passed”: it stands inscribed as a warning for whoever would see, but the distracted soul lets it slip by untaught.

versets.ar: وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّنْ خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ خَلَقَهُنَّ الْعَزِيزُ الْعَلِيمُ
versets.tr: wa-laʾin saʾaltahum man khalaqa s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍa la-yaqūlunna khalaqahunna l-ʿazīzu l-ʿalīmu
versets.trad: If you ask them: “Who created the heavens and the earth?” they will surely say: “The Almighty, the All-Knowing created them.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī notes the contradiction of their hearts: they did confess that God was their Creator and the Maker of the heavens and the earth; what they denied was the message of the prophets, the resurrection, and its possibility. To acknowledge the Creator without worshipping Him or believing in His sending is a knowledge that does not save, for it remains at the surface of confession.

mots.racine: ع ز ز / ع ل م

versets.ar: الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ مَهْدًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ فِيهَا سُبُلًا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ
versets.tr: alladhī jaʿala lakumu l-arḍa mahdan wa-jaʿala lakum fīhā subulan laʿallakum tahtadūna
versets.trad: He who made the earth a cradle for you and traced upon it pathways, that you might be guided;
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī unfolds a striking correspondence: just as He made the earth an abode for bodies, He made bodies an abode for spirits. Creatures are the dwellers of the earth; when the time of souls upon it is fulfilled, God decrees its ruin. Likewise, when the spirits depart entirely from the bodies, He decrees their ruin. The earthly cradle is but a provisional dwelling, an image of the body's transient lodging of the spirit.

mots.def: mahd: bed, cradle, place made level.

versets.ar: وَالَّذِي نَزَّلَ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً بِقَدَرٍ فَأَنشَرْنَا بِهِ بَلْدَةً مَّيْتًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lladhī nazzala mina s-samāʾi māʾan bi-qadarin fa-ansharnā bihi baldatan maytan kadhālika tukhrajūna
versets.trad: and who sends down from the sky water in measure, by which We revive a dead land; even so shall you be brought forth!
versets.commentaire: Beyond the resurrection of bodies, Qushayrī perceives that of hearts: just as He revives the earth by rain, He revives hearts by the beauty of contemplative regard (ḥusn an-naẓar). The water descends “in measure,” neither in excess nor in want; so too the grace that revives the soul comes by an exact wisdom, just enough to make the inner land grow green again.

mots.racine: م و ه / ق د ر
ب ل د / م و ت

versets.ar: وَالَّذِي خَلَقَ الْأَزْوَاجَ كُلَّهَا وَجَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ الْفُلْكِ وَالْأَنْعَامِ مَا تَرْكَبُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lladhī khalaqa l-azwāja kullahā wa-jaʿala lakum mina l-fulki wa-l-anʿāmi mā tarkabūna
versets.trad: and who created all the pairs, and gave you ships and cattle on which you ride,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “the pairs” designate the species and categories of creation. And he transposes the mounts into spiritual states: just as He subjected ship and beast to you, He has made diverse for you all the conditions of the soul — from desire for goods to dread of the threatened punishments, from the fear that makes one forsake faults to the hope that drives one to obedience, coveting the reward — and many other modes among the attributes.

mots.racine: ف ل ك / ن ع م

versets.ar: لِتَسْتَوُوا عَلَىٰ ظُهُورِهِ ثُمَّ تَذْكُرُوا نِعْمَةَ رَبِّكُمْ إِذَا اسْتَوَيْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ وَتَقُولُوا سُبْحَانَ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ لَنَا هَٰذَا وَمَا كُنَّا لَهُ مُقْرِنِينَ
versets.tr: li-tastawū ʿalā ẓuhūrihi thumma tadhkurū niʿmata rabbikum idhā stawaytum ʿalayhi wa-taqūlū subḥāna lladhī sakhkhara lanā hādhā wa-mā kunnā lahu muqrinīna
versets.trad: that you may sit firmly upon their backs, then remember the favor of your Lord when you are settled upon them, and say: “Glory to Him who has subjected this to us, when we ourselves could never have mastered it.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī unfolds a magnificent allusion: as He subjected the ship upon the sea and the beasts for riding, so has He made smooth for the believers the mount of divine enabling (tawfīq) that bears them to the carpet of obedience; He made smooth for the aspirants the mount of longing that bears them to the courtyards of generosity; and for the gnostics the mount of high resolves, until they kneel at the threshold of Glory. There is the term of all: no creature's resolve tears the veils of Power — be he angel brought near, prophet sent, or honored saint; before the onslaughts of Glory every creature vanishes.

mots.racine: س و ي / ظ ه ر
mots.sens: that you may sit upon their backs
mots.def: istawā: to settle upright; ẓahr: back.
muqrin: one who subdues by his own strength.

versets.ar: وَإِنَّا إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا لَمُنقَلِبُونَ
versets.tr: wa-innā ilā rabbinā la-munqalibūna
versets.trad: Truly it is to our Lord that we shall return!”
versets.commentaire: The traveler settled on his mount confesses his final end: every journey is but a figure of the great return. Qushayrī teaches that the aware servant knows that all setting-out here below is ordered to a single term: to return to God; so his outward mount reminds him of the journey of his heart toward the Real. To say “to our Lord we return” is to make of every passage an ascent toward the Origin.

mots.def: inqalaba: to return, turn back toward.

versets.ar: وَجَعَلُوا لَهُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ جُزْءًا ۚ إِنَّ الْإِنسَانَ لَكَفُورٌ مُّبِينٌ
versets.tr: wa-jaʿalū lahu min ʿibādihi juzʾan inna l-insāna la-kafūrun mubīnun
versets.trad: Yet they assign to Him a portion from among His own servants. Truly man is manifestly ungrateful!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: these are the ones who said: “the angels are God's daughters,” thus making the daughters a “portion” of Him, set apart among His creatures. Disgrace and shame upon them for such a saying! To assign to God a part drawn from His creation is to lower Him to the rank of the contingent; and the most manifest ingratitude (kufr) is that which disfigures the very idea of the Lord.

mots.racine: ك ف ر / ب ي ن

versets.ar: أَمِ اتَّخَذَ مِمَّا يَخْلُقُ بَنَاتٍ وَأَصْفَاكُم بِالْبَنِينَ
versets.tr: ami ttakhadha mimmā yakhluqu banātin wa-aṣfākum bi-l-banīna
versets.trad: Has He taken for Himself daughters from what He creates, and chosen sons for you?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī conveys the rebuke: He reproaches them by their own logic — how could they assign Him the daughters and reserve the sons for themselves? Herein is error, for they judge that the Eternal has a child; and ignorance, for they assign Him daughters whose birth they themselves disdain. And then: what fault is there in daughters? How could they decree the angels female whom they never saw created? All this is but forbidden error.

mots.racine: ص ف و / ب ن ي
mots.def: aṣfā: to single out; banūn: sons.

versets.ar: وَإِذَا بُشِّرَ أَحَدُهُم بِمَا ضَرَبَ لِلرَّحْمَٰنِ مَثَلًا ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُ مُسْوَدًّا وَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ
versets.tr: wa-idhā bushshira aḥaduhum bimā ḍaraba li-r-raḥmāni mathalan ẓalla wajhuhu muswaddan wa-huwa kaẓīmun
versets.trad: Yet when one of them is given glad tidings of that [a daughter] which he likens to the Compassionate, his face darkens and he is choked with grief:
versets.commentaire: The contrast is biting: what they despise for themselves — the daughter — they dare assign to God. Qushayrī lays bare the double offense: they ascribe to Him what afflicts them, and their heart chokes with vexation (kaẓīm) at the announcement of a daughter whom they themselves raised up to the Compassionate. The face that blackens betrays the lie of the heart.

mots.racine: ظ ل ل / و ج ه / س و د

mots.def: kaẓīm: one who swallows his rage, stifled with sorrow.

versets.ar: أَوَمَن يُنَشَّأُ فِي الْحِلْيَةِ وَهُوَ فِي الْخِصَامِ غَيْرُ مُبِينٍ
versets.tr: awa-man yunashshaʾu fī l-ḥilyati wa-huwa fī l-khiṣāmi ghayru mubīnin
versets.trad: “What — a being reared amid trinkets and unable, in dispute, to make itself clear!”
versets.commentaire: The verse turns their own reasoning against them: they disdain her who grows up “amid ornaments” and stays helpless in dispute, and it is she they dare assign to God. Qushayrī lets it be understood that the reproach lies not in the creature but in the warped judgment of those who despise what they then exalt to the Lord. Their contradiction denounces itself.

mots.racine: ن ش أ / ح ل ي
mots.def: nashshaʾa: to rear; ḥilya: ornament, jewelry.

versets.ar: وَجَعَلُوا الْمَلَائِكَةَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ عِبَادُ الرَّحْمَٰنِ إِنَاثًا ۚ أَشَهِدُوا خَلْقَهُمْ ۚ سَتُكْتَبُ شَهَادَتُهُمْ وَيُسْأَلُونَ
versets.tr: wa-jaʿalū l-malāʾikata lladhīna hum ʿibādu r-raḥmāni ināthan a-shahidū khalqahum sa-tuktabu shahādatuhum wa-yusʾalūna
versets.trad: They deem the angels, who are the servants of the Compassionate, to be female. Did they witness their creation? Their testimony shall be written down, and they shall be questioned.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī censures this presumption: how could they decree female the angels whom they never saw created? To assert without witnessing is to lie; and this lie is recorded for the day of reckoning. The servant who knows that his words are written, and that he will be questioned over them, refrains from any speech for which he would fear to answer. False testimony about heaven is paid for at heaven's tribunal.

mots.racine: ك ت ب / ش ه د

versets.ar: وَقَالُوا لَوْ شَاءَ الرَّحْمَٰنُ مَا عَبَدْنَاهُم ۗ مَّا لَهُم بِذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ ۖ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ
versets.tr: wa-qālū law shāʾa r-raḥmānu mā ʿabadnāhum mā lahum bi-dhālika min ʿilmin in hum illā yakhruṣūna
versets.trad: They say: “Had the Compassionate willed, we would not have worshipped them.” Of this they have no knowledge; they do but conjecture.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī unmasks the intent: they said this only in mockery and to dismiss the truth, not out of faith or sincerity; so God replied: “they have no knowledge of it.” Had they said it truthfully, truly believing, their words would not have been blameworthy. To invoke the divine decree to justify one's sin, when one does not believe in it, is not humility but cunning — conjecture dressed as theology.

mots.racine: ش ي أ / ر ح م
mots.def: shāʾa: to will; Raḥmān: the All-Compassionate.
kharaṣa: to surmise, speak at random.

versets.ar: أَمْ آتَيْنَاهُمْ كِتَابًا مِّن قَبْلِهِ فَهُم بِهِ مُسْتَمْسِكُونَ
versets.tr: am ātaynāhum kitāban min qablihi fa-hum bihi mustamsikūna
versets.trad: Or have We given them a Book before this, to which they hold fast?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī settles it: no, it is not so — they have no prior Book on which to rest their worship. Their religion stands neither on revelation nor on proof, but solely on the imitation of the fathers, which never leads to true knowledge. A belief without scriptural foundation is but a hollow support that gives way under the weight of the question.

mots.racine: ك ت ب / ق ب ل
mots.def: a scripture prior to the Qurʾān.

versets.ar: بَلْ قَالُوا إِنَّا وَجَدْنَا آبَاءَنَا عَلَىٰ أُمَّةٍ وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰ آثَارِهِم مُّهْتَدُونَ
versets.tr: bal qālū innā wajadnā ābāʾanā ʿalā ummatin wa-innā ʿalā āthārihim muhtadūna
versets.trad: Not at all! They say: “We found our fathers upon a way, and by their traces we are guided.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sums it up: “we imitate them.” Here is their whole foundation: inherited custom, not received light. To follow the fathers' traces without examining whether they lead to God is to confuse habit with guidance. The true aspirant breaks with passive imitation to seek the truth for himself, under the leading of revelation.

mots.def: umma: here, a common way, manner of life.

mots.racine: أ ث ر / ه د ي

versets.ar: وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ فِي قَرْيَةٍ مِّن نَّذِيرٍ إِلَّا قَالَ مُتْرَفُوهَا إِنَّا وَجَدْنَا آبَاءَنَا عَلَىٰ أُمَّةٍ وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰ آثَارِهِم مُّقْتَدُونَ
versets.tr: wa-kadhālika mā arsalnā min qablika fī qaryatin min nadhīrin illā qāla mutrafūhā innā wajadnā ābāʾanā ʿalā ummatin wa-innā ʿalā āthārihim muqtadūna
versets.trad: Even so, We never sent before you any warner to a town but its affluent ones said: “Indeed we found our fathers upon a way, and in their traces we follow.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī notes that it is the “affluent” (mutrafūn) who lead the refusal: they followed the way of those before them in imitating their forebears, out of habituation to the conduct and custom in which they had grown drowsy. Abundance of goods dulls the soul and makes it averse to the call; material ease becomes the thickest of veils before the truth.

mots.def: mutraf: one who lives in luxury and opulence.
iqtadā: to take as a model, follow.

versets.ar: قَالَ أَوَلَوْ جِئْتُكُم بِأَهْدَىٰ مِمَّا وَجَدتُّمْ عَلَيْهِ آبَاءَكُمْ ۖ قَالُوا إِنَّا بِمَا أُرْسِلْتُم بِهِ كَافِرُونَ
versets.tr: qāla awa-law jiʾtukum bi-ahdā mimmā wajadtum ʿalayhi ābāʾakum qālū innā bimā ursiltum bihi kāfirūna
versets.trad: He said: “Even if I bring you better guidance than what you found your fathers upon?” They replied: “We disbelieve in the message you are sent with.”
versets.commentaire: The warner offers them better than the fathers' inheritance, and they answer with sheer denial. Qushayrī: his word took no hold in them, his exhortation availed them nothing, and they persisted in their denial. When the soul braces itself upon custom, even a higher guidance does not shake it: it prefers the familiar error to the new truth.

mots.def: kāfir: one who refuses to believe.

versets.ar: فَانتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ ۖ فَانظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ
versets.tr: fa-ntaqamnā minhum fa-nẓur kayfa kāna ʿāqibatu l-mukadhdhibīna
versets.trad: So We took vengeance on them. See, then, what was the end of the deniers.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: thus the Real — glory to Him — took vengeance on them as He had done with those before them. The verse invites contemplation: “see, then” — the end of the deniers is a mirror held up to every heart. To ponder the fate of the rebellious is a path of return: what the word does not teach, the example of chastisement engraves.

mots.racine: ع ق ب / ك ذ ب
mots.def: ʿāqiba: final outcome; mukadhdhib: one who cries lies.

versets.ar: وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَاهِيمُ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِ إِنَّنِي بَرَاءٌ مِّمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ
versets.tr: wa-idh qāla ibrāhīmu li-abīhi wa-qawmihi innanī barāʾun mimmā taʿbudūna
versets.trad: Remember when Abraham said to his father and his people: “I disown what you worship,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī relates that Abraham, having called his father and his people to God and His Oneness, received from them nothing but denial; so he disowned them all. The disavowal (barāʾa) of false deities is the obverse of attachment to the Real: one unites with God only by detaching from all one worshipped besides Him. The way begins with a “no” before the great “yes.”

versets.ar: إِلَّا الَّذِي فَطَرَنِي فَإِنَّهُ سَيَهْدِينِ
versets.tr: illā lladhī faṭaranī fa-innahu sa-yahdīni
versets.trad: save Him who created me, for He will surely guide me.”
versets.commentaire: After the disavowal comes the affirmation: worship is due to none but Him who fashioned the being (faṭara). Qushayrī sees in this saying of Abraham the heart of tawḥīd: no guide save Him who gave existence; from Him comes the orientation, as from Him came the creation. He who created me will know how to lead me: faith entrusts the way to the Fashioner.

mots.def: faṭara: to fashion, create from the origin.

versets.ar: وَجَعَلَهَا كَلِمَةً بَاقِيَةً فِي عَقِبِهِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ
versets.tr: wa-jaʿalahā kalimatan bāqiyatan fī ʿaqibihi laʿallahum yarjiʿūna
versets.trad: And He made this an abiding word among his descendants, that they might return.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: God made the word of Oneness an abiding word in his progeny and his people. Abraham's tawḥīd was no isolated instant but an enduring seed, handed down from generation to generation, that hearts might “return” to their origin. The true word, once uttered in sincerity, does not die: it endures as an inheritance of return.

mots.racine: ك ل م / ب ق ي
mots.def: kalima: word; bāqiya: that which endures.

versets.ar: بَلْ مَتَّعْتُ هَٰؤُلَاءِ وَآبَاءَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ جَاءَهُمُ الْحَقُّ وَرَسُولٌ مُّبِينٌ
versets.tr: bal mattaʿtu hāʾulāʾi wa-ābāʾahum ḥattā jāʾahumu l-ḥaqqu wa-rasūlun mubīnun
versets.trad: Indeed, I let these and their fathers enjoy themselves until the Truth came to them, and a manifest Messenger.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: We slackened for a time the reins of their respite, then came Our turn: We overcame them and destroyed them all together. The enjoyment granted was but a delay, not a favor; God lets things ripen before He cuts. The respite of the sinner is not forgetfulness: it is a loosened rope that tightens when the Truth comes.

mots.racine: ح ق ق / ر س ل
mots.sens: the Truth and a manifest Messenger
mots.def: ḥaqq: the Truth; rasūl mubīn: messenger with a clear message.

versets.ar: وَلَمَّا جَاءَهُمُ الْحَقُّ قَالُوا هَٰذَا سِحْرٌ وَإِنَّا بِهِ كَافِرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lammā jāʾahumu l-ḥaqqu qālū hādhā siḥrun wa-innā bihi kāfirūna
versets.trad: Yet when the Truth reached them, they said: “This is magic, and in it we disbelieve!”
versets.commentaire: At the Truth's arrival they respond with the charge of magic. Qushayrī lets it be understood that to call “magic” what surpasses the understanding is the refuge of the soul that will not surrender: it names illusion what it can neither deny nor embrace. The word “sorcery” is the mask that denial lays over the evidence that confounds it.

mots.def: kāfir: one who refuses to believe in it.

versets.ar: وَقَالُوا لَوْلَا نُزِّلَ هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنُ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنَ الْقَرْيَتَيْنِ عَظِيمٍ
versets.tr: wa-qālū lawlā nuzzila hādhā l-qurʾānu ʿalā rajulin mina l-qaryatayni ʿaẓīmin
versets.trad: And they said: “Why was this Qurʾān not sent down upon some great man of the two towns?”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī recalls that they meant a great man of Ṭāʾif or of Mecca, and that was an excess of their ignorance: they measured prophetic election by social rank and wealth. The following verse will confound them. To believe that divine grace follows the prestige of men is to ignore that God chooses whom He wills, with no regard for the world's hierarchies.

mots.sens: why was it not sent down
mots.def: nuzzila: to be revealed, sent down.

mots.racine: ق ر ي / ع ظ م
mots.sens: a great man of the two towns
mots.def: al-qaryatān: Mecca and Ṭāʾif; ʿaẓīm: eminent.

versets.ar: أَهُمْ يَقْسِمُونَ رَحْمَتَ رَبِّكَ ۚ نَحْنُ قَسَمْنَا بَيْنَهُم مَّعِيشَتَهُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا ۚ وَرَفَعْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجَاتٍ لِّيَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُهُم بَعْضًا سُخْرِيًّا ۗ وَرَحْمَتُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ
versets.tr: a-hum yaqsimūna raḥmata rabbika naḥnu qasamnā baynahum maʿīshatahum fī l-ḥayāti d-dunyā wa-rafaʿnā baʿḍahum fawqa baʿḍin darajātin li-yattakhidha baʿḍuhum baʿḍan sukhriyyan wa-raḥmatu rabbika khayrun mimmā yajmaʿūna
versets.trad: Is it they who apportion the mercy of your Lord? It is We who have apportioned among them their livelihood in the life of this world, and raised some of them above others in degrees, so that some may take others into service. But the mercy of your Lord is better than what they amass.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: Is it they who apportion the mercy of your Lord — that is, election to prophethood? Would God's choice follow their whim? How wretched their judgment! And he rises to the essential allusion: the Real has entrusted to no one the apportioning of bliss and wretchedness; the rejected is so by His decree, and the accepted among His servants is the one He willed and received — not for any cause or acquired merit. Then: to the rich, the experience of the gift, and they gave thanks; to the poor, the contemplation of the Giver Himself, and they praised. The rich saw the gift and were distracted by it; the poor heard “It is We” and were absorbed in it.

mots.racine: ق س م / ر ح م
mots.sens: apportion the mercy of your Lord

mots.def: sukhriyy: subjection, being put to mutual service.

versets.ar: وَلَوْلَا أَن يَكُونَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً لَّجَعَلْنَا لِمَن يَكْفُرُ بِالرَّحْمَٰنِ لِبُيُوتِهِمْ سُقُفًا مِّن فِضَّةٍ وَمَعَارِجَ عَلَيْهَا يَظْهَرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lawlā an yakūna n-nāsu ummatan wāḥidatan la-jaʿalnā li-man yakfuru bi-r-raḥmāni li-buyūtihim suqufan min fiḍḍatin wa-maʿārija ʿalayhā yaẓharūna
versets.trad: Were it not that mankind would become a single community [of disbelief], We would have made for those who deny the Compassionate roofs of silver for their houses, and stairways by which to mount,
versets.commentaire: Here is the heart of the sūra according to Qushayrī: the meaning of the verse is that the world has no worth in Our sight. As for him who abides with Us, if We poured the whole world upon him, it would not make up for his deprivation. And were it not for the temptation of the believers' hearts, We would have made for the houses of the deniers roofs of silver and stairways of silver — and had We done so, what We gave would have no worth, for the whole world is of no value with Us. That God would lavish luxury upon the deniers proves how negligible this world is to Him.

mots.racine: س ق ف / ف ض ض

mots.def: miʿrāj (pl. maʿārij): step, ladder for ascending.

versets.ar: وَلِبُيُوتِهِمْ أَبْوَابًا وَسُرُرًا عَلَيْهَا يَتَّكِئُونَ
versets.tr: wa-li-buyūtihim abwāban wa-sururan ʿalayhā yattakiʾūna
versets.trad: and doors for their houses, and couches upon which to recline,
versets.commentaire: The luxury unfolds: silver doors, couches to recline upon. Qushayrī ties all this to the preceding verse — so many splendors that God would grant the deniers precisely because they are worth nothing in His sight. Reclining on the cushions of the world is but a deceptive support; the true believer does not rest his heart upon it, knowing the frailty of all that collapses.

mots.def: sarīr (pl. surur): bed, divan to recline on.

versets.ar: وَزُخْرُفًا ۚ وَإِن كُلُّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمَّا مَتَاعُ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا ۚ وَالْآخِرَةُ عِندَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ
versets.tr: wa-zukhrufan wa-in kullu dhālika lammā matāʿu l-ḥayāti d-dunyā wa-l-ākhiratu ʿinda rabbika li-l-muttaqīna
versets.trad: and gold ornaments. Yet all this is but the enjoyment of the life of this world, while the Hereafter, with your Lord, is for the godfearing.
versets.commentaire: Here is the word that names the sūra: zukhruf, the gold ornament. And at once its relativization: all this is but “the enjoyment of the life of this world.” Qushayrī teaches that the gold and adornment of the world, however lavishly bestowed, remain worthless before God; only the Hereafter, reserved for the godfearing, has real value. The dazzling ornament is but a surface gleam; the true treasure is kept “with your Lord.”

mots.racine: أ خ ر / و ق ي
mots.def: ākhira: the hereafter; muttaqī: one who fears God.

versets.ar: وَمَن يَعْشُ عَن ذِكْرِ الرَّحْمَٰنِ نُقَيِّضْ لَهُ شَيْطَانًا فَهُوَ لَهُ قَرِينٌ
versets.tr: wa-man yaʿshu ʿan dhikri r-raḥmāni nuqayyiḍ lahu shayṭānan fa-huwa lahu qarīnun
versets.trad: Whoever turns blind to the remembrance of the Compassionate, We assign to him a devil, who becomes his close companion.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī makes of this verse a lesson on solitude with God: whoever has not known the worth of intimate retreat with God, and has turned from His remembrance to abandon himself to base thoughts, God assigns to him one who occupies him away from Him — such is the recompense of one who neglects the courtesy (adab) of the khalwa. And he adds: “the worst of devils is your own soul”; the servant who fails to know the value of his heart's emptiness, and follows his passion, remains captive to his desire, from which he is freed only with great difficulty.

mots.racine: ع ش و / ذ ك ر
mots.sens: turns blind to the remembrance of the Compassionate
mots.def: ʿashā: to be dim of sight, to turn away.

versets.ar: وَإِنَّهُمْ لَيَصُدُّونَهُمْ عَنِ السَّبِيلِ وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ
versets.tr: wa-innahum la-yaṣuddūnahum ʿani s-sabīli wa-yaḥsabūna annahum muhtadūna
versets.trad: And surely they turn them away from the path, while they think themselves rightly guided,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī describes the mechanism: he whose soul has made fair to him a thing he imagines right, then who draws his companion to approve him in his error while claiming to be in the right — that one harms himself and harms others. The devil's greatest danger is not the patent evil but the evil that disguises itself as guidance: to believe one walks straight while going astray.

mots.racine: ص د د / س ب ل
mots.sens: they turn them from the path
mots.def: ṣadda: to turn away, divert; sabīl: the way.

mots.racine: ح س ب / ه د ي

versets.ar: حَتَّىٰ إِذَا جَاءَنَا قَالَ يَا لَيْتَ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكَ بُعْدَ الْمَشْرِقَيْنِ فَبِئْسَ الْقَرِينُ
versets.tr: ḥattā idhā jāʾanā qāla yā layta baynī wa-baynaka buʿda l-mashriqayni fa-biʾsa l-qarīnu
versets.trad: until, when such a one comes to Us, he says [to his companion]: “Would that between me and you there were the distance of the two Easts! What a wretched companion!”
versets.commentaire: When the veil falls, the friendship built on error reverses into hatred. Qushayrī: on the day the veil is lifted, the man recognizes his companion's treachery and repents of having kept his company; he cries: “woe to me, would that I had never taken so-and-so for a friend!” and: “would that between you and me there were the distance of the two Easts!” But this regret no longer avails, for the time is past. Company forged outside God always ends in remorse.

mots.racine: ب ع د / ش ر ق
mots.sens: the distance of the two Easts
mots.def: al-mashriqān: the two risings (East and West), image of utmost distance.

mots.racine: ب أ س / ق ر ن
mots.def: biʾsa: verb of blame; qarīn: companion.

versets.ar: وَلَن يَنفَعَكُمُ الْيَوْمَ إِذ ظَّلَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ فِي الْعَذَابِ مُشْتَرِكُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lan yanfaʿakumu l-yawma idh ẓalamtum annakum fī l-ʿadhābi mushtarikūna
versets.trad: But it will not avail you this Day, since you did wrong, that you share in the chastisement.
versets.commentaire: Regret comes too late. Qushayrī recalls that this remorse no longer profits, for the time is gone; the companion in error now shares the penalty. To share the chastisement without being able to share it for relief: such is the bitter solidarity of the partners in evil, where the fellowship of fault becomes a fellowship of suffering.

mots.def: mushtarik: one who shares, partakes together.

versets.ar: أَفَأَنتَ تُسْمِعُ الصُّمَّ أَوْ تَهْدِي الْعُمْيَ وَمَن كَانَ فِي ضَلَالٍ مُّبِينٍ
versets.tr: afa-anta tusmiʿu ṣ-ṣumma aw tahdī l-ʿumya wa-man kāna fī ḍalālin mubīnin
versets.trad: Can you, then, make the deaf hear, or guide the blind and one who is in manifest error?
versets.commentaire: The question carries a negation, says Qushayrī: you cannot guide one whose insight We have sealed, whose own rectitude We have veiled from him, and into the ears of whose understanding We have poured the lead of wretchedness and deprivation — how then would you make him hear? The guidance of hearts is not in the preacher's power: it belongs to God alone, who opens or seals.

mots.racine: س م ع / ص م م
mots.def: asmaʿa: to make hear; aṣamm (pl. ṣumm): deaf.

mots.racine: ه د ي / ع م ي

versets.ar: فَإِمَّا نَذْهَبَنَّ بِكَ فَإِنَّا مِنْهُم مُّنتَقِمُونَ
versets.tr: fa-immā nadhhabanna bika fa-innā minhum muntaqimūna
versets.trad: Whether We take you away, We shall surely take vengeance on them,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: if your term ends without your being granted to see what We threaten them with, do not imagine that the truthfulness of Our word is mixed with any lie: what We have informed you of will inevitably come to pass. The divine promise does not depend on what the servant sees with his eyes: it is fulfilled according to the decree, whether he witnesses it or not.

mots.def: adhhaba bi: to take away, cause to depart.

versets.ar: أَوْ نُرِيَنَّكَ الَّذِي وَعَدْنَاهُمْ فَإِنَّا عَلَيْهِم مُّقْتَدِرُونَ
versets.tr: aw nuriyannaka lladhī waʿadnāhum fa-innā ʿalayhim muqtadirūna
versets.trad: or We show you what We have promised them, for We have full power over them.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī observes that this verse holds the servant between fear and hope, specifying nothing, so that he may remain a witness of the decree, God doing what He wills. Whether the outcome is shown to him or not, the believer remains a spectator of destiny, his heart suspended upon the sovereign will alone. God's power over them is entire: none escapes His grip.

mots.def: arā: to show, cause to see.
muqtadir: one who has full power.

versets.ar: فَاسْتَمْسِكْ بِالَّذِي أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ
versets.tr: fa-stamsik bi-lladhī ūḥiya ilayka innaka ʿalā ṣirāṭin mustaqīmin
versets.trad: So hold fast to what is revealed to you; truly you are on a straight path!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī distills the injunction: strive without slackening, entrust yourself to God without faltering, stand where you are commanded, and be certain that you are on the straight path. Effort (ijtihād) without negligence and surrender (tawakkul) without lukewarmness are the two wings of the wayfarer. Firmness in revelation is itself the sign and the warrant of the path's straightness.

mots.racine: ص ر ط / ق و م

versets.ar: وَإِنَّهُ لَذِكْرٌ لَّكَ وَلِقَوْمِكَ ۖ وَسَوْفَ تُسْأَلُونَ
versets.tr: wa-innahu la-dhikrun laka wa-li-qawmika wa-sawfa tusʾalūna
versets.trad: And truly it is a reminder for you and for your people; and you shall be questioned.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: this Qurʾān is for you a reminder, that is, an honor, a fair renown, and the merit of a high rank. The “dhikr” is here both remembrance and nobility. But honor binds: “you shall be questioned.” To receive the Word is to bear a dignity for which one must answer — grace and responsibility go together.

mots.def: dhikr: reminder; here, glory and nobility.
saʾala: to question, call to account.

versets.ar: وَاسْأَلْ مَنْ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رُّسُلِنَا أَجَعَلْنَا مِن دُونِ الرَّحْمَٰنِ آلِهَةً يُعْبَدُونَ
versets.tr: wa-sʾal man arsalnā min qablika min rusulinā a-jaʿalnā min dūni r-raḥmāni ālihatan yuʿbadūna
versets.trad: Ask those of Our messengers whom We sent before you: did We ever appoint, besides the Compassionate, gods to be worshipped?
themes: the unity of the prophetic message
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī relates the tradition of the Ascension: the spirits of the prophets were gathered on the night of the Ascent, and it was said to the Prophet: “ask them: did We ever command the worship of any other than Us?” But the Prophet did not doubt, and asked nothing. All the messengers preached but one God: tawḥīd is the single thread linking every revelation. Oneness has no history: it is the very substance of all prophecy.

mots.racine: د و ن / أ ل ه
mots.def: ilāh (pl. āliha): deity; min dūn: apart from.

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِآيَاتِنَا إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَئِهِ فَقَالَ إِنِّي رَسُولُ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-laqad arsalnā mūsā bi-āyātinā ilā firʿawna wa-malaʾihi fa-qāla innī rasūlu rabbi l-ʿālamīna
versets.trad: We did send Moses, with Our signs, to Pharaoh and his chiefs. He said: “I am the Messenger of the Lord of the worlds.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sums it up: We sent him with Our proofs, a dazzling and irresistible demonstration, with the miracles, to Pharaoh and his Coptic people; and he was met with mockery and laughter. Moses presents himself not in his own name but as “Messenger of the Lord of the worlds”: the messenger effaces himself behind the One who sends him. The strength of the prophet is not his person but the mission he bears.

mots.racine: ر س ل / ر ب ب / ع ل م
mots.sens: Messenger of the Lord of the worlds
mots.def: rasūl: messenger; rabb al-ʿālamīn: Lord of the worlds.

versets.ar: فَلَمَّا جَاءَهُم بِآيَاتِنَا إِذَا هُم مِّنْهَا يَضْحَكُونَ
versets.tr: fa-lammā jāʾahum bi-āyātinā idhā hum minhā yaḍḥakūna
versets.trad: But when he came to them with Our signs, behold, they laughed at them,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī underlines the contrast: he was met with laughter and denial. Faced with signs that should have confounded them, they choose ridicule. Laughter is here the armor of refusal: to mock the sign is to attempt to reduce it to nothing rather than receive its light. The soul that laughs at the miracle deprives itself of the very grace the miracle bore.

mots.racine: ج ي أ / أ ي ي
mots.sens: came to them with Our signs
mots.def: jāʾa bi: to bring, come with.

versets.ar: وَمَا نُرِيهِم مِّنْ آيَةٍ إِلَّا هِيَ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ أُخْتِهَا ۖ وَأَخَذْنَاهُم بِالْعَذَابِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ
versets.tr: wa-mā nurīhim min āyatin illā hiya akbaru min ukhtihā wa-akhadhnāhum bi-l-ʿadhābi laʿallahum yarjiʿūna
versets.trad: though We showed them no sign but it was greater than the one before it. So We seized them with chastisement, that they might return.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: God produced for them no proof but it was clearer than the one before; yet they met each only with a harshness more savage than before. The greater the sign, the harder their denial: the closed heart turns every light into a reason to sink deeper. The chastisement itself is offered as a chance for return, not as an end.

mots.racine: ك ب ر / أ خ و
mots.def: ukht: sister, here the preceding sign.

versets.ar: وَقَالُوا يَا أَيُّهَ السَّاحِرُ ادْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ بِمَا عَهِدَ عِندَكَ إِنَّنَا لَمُهْتَدُونَ
versets.tr: wa-qālū yā ayyuha s-sāḥiru dʿu lanā rabbaka bimā ʿahida ʿindaka innanā la-muhtadūna
versets.trad: They said: “O magician! Call upon your Lord for us, by the covenant He has made with you. Then we shall surely be guided!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: when the affliction bit them, they said: “O magician, call upon your Lord for us to lift the trial from us, and we will believe in you.” They still call Moses “magician” in the very prayer with which they implore him: their heart stays closed even in distress. The promise wrung out by fear is no faith: it is a bargain broken the moment the danger passes.

mots.sens: by the covenant made with you

versets.ar: فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُمُ الْعَذَابَ إِذَا هُمْ يَنكُثُونَ
versets.tr: fa-lammā kashafnā ʿanhumu l-ʿadhāba idhā hum yankuthūna
versets.trad: But as soon as We lifted the chastisement from them, behold, they broke their word.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: Moses implored, God lifted the trial from them, and they returned to their disbelief, breaking their covenant. No sooner is the torment lifted than the oath vanishes: faith born of fear does not outlast the fear. Whoever turns to God only under the blow of affliction betrays Him the moment relief comes; only faith born of longing, not of dread, remains faithful.

mots.racine: ك ش ف / ع ذ ب
mots.sens: We lifted from them the chastisement

versets.ar: وَنَادَىٰ فِرْعَوْنُ فِي قَوْمِهِ قَالَ يَا قَوْمِ أَلَيْسَ لِي مُلْكُ مِصْرَ وَهَٰذِهِ الْأَنْهَارُ تَجْرِي مِن تَحْتِي ۖ أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-nādā firʿawnu fī qawmihi qāla yā qawmi a-laysa lī mulku miṣra wa-hādhihi l-anhāru tajrī min taḥtī afa-lā tubṣirūna
versets.trad: And Pharaoh proclaimed among his people: “O my people! Is not the kingdom of Egypt mine, and these rivers flowing beneath me? Do you not see?
themes: ruin through the object of pride
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī draws from Pharaoh's vanity an implacable spiritual law: he gloried in the kingdom of Egypt, and the Nile flowed at his command! Yet in this very thing was his ruin — that it might be known that whoever glories in anything other than God, his destruction and death come to him through that very thing. The river he boasted of will swallow him: the object of pride becomes the instrument of chastisement.

mots.racine: ن ه ر / ج ر ي
mots.def: nahr (pl. anhār): river; jarā: to flow.

versets.ar: أَمْ أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِّنْ هَٰذَا الَّذِي هُوَ مَهِينٌ وَلَا يَكَادُ يُبِينُ
versets.tr: am anā khayrun min hādhā lladhī huwa mahīnun wa-lā yakādu yubīnu
versets.trad: Am I not better than this contemptible man who can hardly make himself clear?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī draws here a second law: Pharaoh belittled Moses and his message and reproached him with poverty; so God gave Moses power over him, and his ruin came by the very hands of the one he despised. For no one belittles another but God gives that other power over him. Contempt for the weak recoils into the dominion of the despised: what the proud man disdains becomes the cause of his fall.

mots.racine: ك و د / ب ي ن

versets.ar: فَلَوْلَا أُلْقِيَ عَلَيْهِ أَسْوِرَةٌ مِّن ذَهَبٍ أَوْ جَاءَ مَعَهُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ مُقْتَرِنِينَ
versets.tr: fa-lawlā ulqiya ʿalayhi aswiratun min dhahabin aw jāʾa maʿahu l-malāʾikatu muqtarinīna
versets.trad: Why have no bracelets of gold been cast upon him, or angels come with him in attendance?”
versets.commentaire: Pharaoh measures dignity by bracelets of gold and a retinue — as the Meccans measured prophethood by rank. Qushayrī lets it be understood that true greatness is adorned neither with gold nor escort; it is the blind, proud man who demands tokens of pomp where God has placed the light of the mission. The gold Pharaoh demands is the same vain zukhruf that weighs nothing before God.

mots.racine: س و ر / ذ ه ب

mots.def: muqtarin: joined, accompanying in a train.

versets.ar: فَاسْتَخَفَّ قَوْمَهُ فَأَطَاعُوهُ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا قَوْمًا فَاسِقِينَ
versets.tr: fa-stakhaffa qawmahu fa-aṭāʿūhu innahum kānū qawman fāsiqīna
versets.trad: Thus he made his people compliant, and they obeyed him; truly they were a depraved people.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī distinguishes two obediences: they obeyed him with an obedience of dread; and obedience born of dread is never sincere. Obedience is truthful only when it springs from desire (raghba). The people subjected to Pharaoh act not from love but from fear; and all submission founded on terror is hollow. Only obedience born of attraction to God bears the seal of sincerity.

mots.def: istakhaffa: to sway, render frivolous and pliant.
fāsiq: one who departs from obedience, dissolute.

versets.ar: فَلَمَّا آسَفُونَا انتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ فَأَغْرَقْنَاهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ
versets.tr: fa-lammā āsafūnā ntaqamnā minhum fa-aghraqnāhum ajmaʿīna
versets.trad: So when they roused Our wrath, We took vengeance on them and drowned them all together.
versets.commentaire: On the word “they roused Us to wrath,” Qushayrī delivers a fine doctrine of “gathering” (jamʿ): “they angered Us” means: they angered Our beloved ones, and We took vengeance for them. God refers to Himself the offense done to His friends — as in the ḥadīth: “I was sick and you did not visit Me.” To wrong the saint is to wrong God; to honor His friend is to honor Him. In the station of jamʿ, the act done to the beloved servant is referred to the Lord Himself.

mots.racine: غ ر ق / ج م ع
mots.def: aghraqa: to drown; ajmaʿīn: all together.

versets.ar: فَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ سَلَفًا وَمَثَلًا لِّلْآخِرِينَ
versets.tr: fa-jaʿalnāhum salafan wa-mathalan li-l-ākhirīna
versets.trad: We made them a thing of the past and an example for those to come.
versets.commentaire: The fate of Pharaoh becomes a lesson for the ages. What was chastisement for them becomes an “example” (mathal) for those who come after. Qushayrī recalls elsewhere that to ponder the fate of the rebellious is a path of return: God inscribes the end of tyrants in the memory of men so that the warning may cross time. The past becomes a mirror for whoever knows how to look.

mots.def: salaf: predecessors, what has gone before.

mots.racine: م ث ل / أ خ ر
mots.def: mathal: example, model; ākhirīn: those who come after.

versets.ar: وَلَمَّا ضُرِبَ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ مَثَلًا إِذَا قَوْمُكَ مِنْهُ يَصِدُّونَ
versets.tr: wa-lammā ḍuriba bnu maryama mathalan idhā qawmuka minhu yaṣiddūna
versets.trad: And when the son of Mary is offered as an example, behold, your people clamor against it,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī recalls that the example offered is this: “Jesus is, before God, like Adam: He created him without a father, as He created Adam without parents.” And they denied by this sign. Rather than recognize the creative power, they turn away from it with shouting. The luminous example becomes for the wrangling heart a pretext for dispute, not a door of understanding.

mots.racine: ض ر ب / م ث ل
mots.def: ḍaraba mathalan: to give an example, a parable.
ṣadda: to turn away, raise an outcry.

versets.ar: وَقَالُوا أَآلِهَتُنَا خَيْرٌ أَمْ هُوَ ۚ مَا ضَرَبُوهُ لَكَ إِلَّا جَدَلًا ۚ بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ خَصِمُونَ
versets.tr: wa-qālū a-ālihatunā khayrun am huwa mā ḍarabūhu laka illā jadalan bal hum qawmun khaṣimūna
versets.trad: saying: “Are our gods not better than he?” They cite him to you only to dispute; indeed they are a contentious people.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī dismantles their ruse: they raise it to you only for chicanery — for they thought to corner you: if you say “your gods are better,” you admit they deserve worship; if you say “Jesus is better,” you grant that he is worthy of adoration. But their question was not to learn. The Prophet cuts through: Jesus is better than your idols, yet none is worthy of worship but God. The dispute that does not seek the truth is but a game of the recalcitrant soul.

mots.racine: أ ل ه / خ ي ر

versets.ar: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا عَبْدٌ أَنْعَمْنَا عَلَيْهِ وَجَعَلْنَاهُ مَثَلًا لِّبَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ
versets.tr: in huwa illā ʿabdun anʿamnā ʿalayhi wa-jaʿalnāhu mathalan li-banī isrāʾīla
versets.trad: He [Jesus] was only a servant on whom We bestowed Our grace, and whom We made an example for the Children of Israel.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī cuts cleanly: Jesus was only a servant whom We graced with prophethood. All of Jesus's dignity rests in this word: “servant” (ʿabd). His greatness lies not in any divine nature but in the perfection of his servanthood and the gracious gift he received. The highest rank before God is not to cease being a servant, but to be one fully — perfect servanthood is the supreme honor.

mots.racine: ع ب د / ن ع م
mots.def: ʿabd: servant; anʿama: to bestow favor.

mots.sens: an example for the Children of Israel

versets.ar: وَلَوْ نَشَاءُ لَجَعَلْنَا مِنكُم مَّلَائِكَةً فِي الْأَرْضِ يَخْلُفُونَ
versets.tr: wa-law nashāʾu la-jaʿalnā minkum malāʾikatan fī l-arḍi yakhlufūna
versets.trad: Had We willed, We could have made among you angels to succeed one another on the earth.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: had We willed, We would have sent down angels from heaven to dwell on the earth in your stead. The verse recalls that God needs no one and that no creature is indispensable to Him. Man is on earth only by pure favor, not by necessity; his place could be held by others — a radical humility of the servant before the sovereign freedom of the Creator.

mots.racine: م ل ك / أ ر ض

versets.ar: وَإِنَّهُ لَعِلْمٌ لِّلسَّاعَةِ فَلَا تَمْتَرُنَّ بِهَا وَاتَّبِعُونِ ۚ هَٰذَا صِرَاطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ
versets.tr: wa-innahu la-ʿilmun li-s-sāʿati fa-lā tamtarunna bihā wa-ttabiʿūni hādhā ṣirāṭun mustaqīmun
versets.trad: And truly he is a sign of the Hour; so do not doubt it, but follow Me. This is a straight path!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reports the interpretation: “he is a sign of the Hour” refers to Jesus: when God brings him down from heaven, he will be the mark of the Hour; doubt not, then, his descent at the approach of the Resurrection. Jesus's descent announces the end of times. Beyond the sign, the injunction remains: “follow Me, this is the straight path” — the ultimate truth is received in obedience, not in doubt.

mots.racine: ع ل م / س و ع
mots.def: ʿilm: knowledge, sign; sāʿa: the final Hour.

versets.ar: وَلَا يَصُدَّنَّكُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ ۖ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ
versets.tr: wa-lā yaṣuddannakumu sh-shayṭānu innahu lakum ʿaduwwun mubīnun
versets.trad: Let not Satan turn you away; he is for you a manifest enemy.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: let not Satan turn you from faith in the Hour nor from following My guidance. The devil aims not only at gross sin but at the subtle deflection from the way. His weapon is to make the wayfarer's step swerve by making him take error for rectitude; his ruse is all the more dangerous for being hidden. To call him a “manifest enemy” is already a protection: to know the adversary is to begin to guard against him.

mots.sens: let him not turn you away

mots.racine: ع د و / ب ي ن

versets.ar: وَلَمَّا جَاءَ عِيسَىٰ بِالْبَيِّنَاتِ قَالَ قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِالْحِكْمَةِ وَلِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُم بَعْضَ الَّذِي تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ ۖ فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ
versets.tr: wa-lammā jāʾa ʿīsā bi-l-bayyināti qāla qad jiʾtukum bi-l-ḥikmati wa-li-ubayyina lakum baʿḍa lladhī takhtalifūna fīhi fa-ttaqū llāha wa-aṭīʿūni
versets.trad: When Jesus came with the clear proofs, he said: “I have come to you with wisdom, and to make clear to you some of that over which you differ. So fear God and obey me!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: Jesus came to his people with the clear laws and called them to the religion of God, but they formed factions against him. His mission was to bring “wisdom” (ḥikma) and to dispel some of their differences. The call reduces to two words: “fear God and obey me.” True wisdom does not end in knowledge but in reverential fear and the obedience that flows from it.

versets.ar: إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ رَبِّي وَرَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُوهُ ۚ هَٰذَا صِرَاطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ
versets.tr: inna llāha huwa rabbī wa-rabbukum fa-ʿbudūhu hādhā ṣirāṭun mustaqīmun
versets.trad: Truly God is my Lord and your Lord. So worship Him! This is a straight path!”
versets.commentaire: Jesus proclaims pure servanthood: “my Lord and your Lord.” He ranks himself among the servants, not above them. Qushayrī sees in this saying the seal of the message: the straight path is the worship of the one God, Lord of all alike; no intermediary stands in for Him, and the messenger himself is but a worshipper among worshippers. To acknowledge the common Lord is to walk the straight path.

versets.ar: فَاخْتَلَفَ الْأَحْزَابُ مِن بَيْنِهِمْ ۖ فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْ عَذَابِ يَوْمٍ أَلِيمٍ
versets.tr: fa-khtalafa l-aḥzābu min baynihim fa-waylun li-lladhīna ẓalamū min ʿadhābi yawmin alīmin
versets.trad: Then the factions differed among themselves. Woe to the wrongdoers from the chastisement of a painful Day!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī evokes the factions that divided over Jesus. After the clarity of his message of Oneness, men tore themselves into sects: the pure word was fractured by the passions. Where the prophet had brought but one way, hearts in quest of dominion made of it so many parties; and the woe announced strikes precisely those who, through injustice, broke the unity of the truth.

mots.racine: خ ل ف / ح ز ب

mots.def: wayl: woe; ẓalama: to do wrong.

versets.ar: هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا السَّاعَةَ أَن تَأْتِيَهُم بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ
versets.tr: hal yanẓurūna illā s-sāʿata an taʾtiyahum baghtatan wa-hum lā yashʿurūna
versets.trad: Do they await anything but the Hour, that it should come upon them suddenly, while they perceive it not?
versets.commentaire: The Hour will come “suddenly” (baghtatan), while they are distracted. Qushayrī teaches elsewhere that it does not befit the servant to be deceived by the length of his security, for the outcomes are not guaranteed. Heedlessness is the trap: to believe one has time is already to be taken by surprise. The wise live each instant as though it were the last, for none knows the moment of the irruption.

versets.ar: الْأَخِلَّاءُ يَوْمَئِذٍ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ إِلَّا الْمُتَّقِينَ
versets.tr: al-akhillāʾu yawmaʾidhin baʿḍuhum li-baʿḍin ʿaduwwun illā l-muttaqīna
versets.trad: On that Day, close friends will become enemies one to another, save the godfearing.
versets.commentaire: Here is one of the summits of the sūra. Qushayrī: what was not for God ends in ruin. Friends bound according to passion become enemies one of another, disowning each other; none profits any. But friends in God intercede for one another and plead each for the other: these are the godfearing whom God excepts. And he sets out the conditions of true friendship (khulla): that neither use the other for his worldly affairs nor draw profit from him, that the companionship be pure for God with no interest of this world, that the mutual contentment be for God alone, without complaisance — each loving the other to the measure of what he sees in him of acceptance of the way of God.

mots.def: muttaqī: one who fears God, godfearing.

versets.ar: يَا عِبَادِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْكُمُ الْيَوْمَ وَلَا أَنتُمْ تَحْزَنُونَ
versets.tr: yā ʿibādi lā khawfun ʿalaykumu l-yawma wa-lā antum taḥzanūna
versets.trad: O My servants! No fear shall be upon you this Day, nor shall you grieve,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī places in this address a total consolation: it will be said to them tomorrow: “O My servants, no fear upon you this Day” — regarding what others meet of the terrors — “nor shall you grieve” for the works you fell short of. As for the sins, We have forgiven them; as for the terrors, We have spared you them; as for the wrongs, We have settled them. A threefold easing: pardon of faults, abolition of terrors, justice rendered.

mots.racine: خ و ف / ح ز ن

versets.ar: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِآيَاتِنَا وَكَانُوا مُسْلِمِينَ
versets.tr: alladhīna āmanū bi-āyātinā wa-kānū muslimīna
versets.trad: [you] who believed in Our signs and were submitted (muslimūn).
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī describes the scene with subtlety: when the herald proclaims “O My servants,” all hope and say “we are His servants”; but when he adds “those who believed in Our signs and were submitted,” the deniers despair and the hope of the submitted is strengthened. The qualification narrows the promise: the Day's security is reserved for those who joined faith to surrender (islām). The wide call becomes a precise promise for the surrendered hearts.

mots.racine: أ م ن / أ ي ي
mots.def: āmana: to believe; āyāt: signs, verses.
muslim: one who surrenders to God.

versets.ar: ادْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ أَنتُمْ وَأَزْوَاجُكُمْ تُحْبَرُونَ
versets.tr: udkhulū l-jannata antum wa-azwājukum tuḥbarūna
versets.trad: Enter the Garden, you and your spouses, to be made joyful!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī glosses the term “tuḥbarūn”: you shall be gladdened in the gardens of Paradise, you shall take your delight there; and it is also said: gladdened by the sweetness of spiritual audition (samāʿ). The joy promised is not only that of the senses but of an exultation that appears on the faces. For the enamored souls, the supreme felicity includes the rapture of spiritual listening, a ravishing of the heart beyond all pleasures.

mots.racine: د خ ل / ج ن ن

mots.def: ḥubira: to be filled with radiant joy.

versets.ar: يُطَافُ عَلَيْهِم بِصِحَافٍ مِّن ذَهَبٍ وَأَكْوَابٍ ۖ وَفِيهَا مَا تَشْتَهِيهِ الْأَنفُسُ وَتَلَذُّ الْأَعْيُنُ ۖ وَأَنتُمْ فِيهَا خَالِدُونَ
versets.tr: yuṭāfu ʿalayhim bi-ṣiḥāfin min dhahabin wa-akwābin wa-fīhā mā tashtahīhi l-anfusu wa-taladhdhu l-aʿyunu wa-antum fīhā khālidūna
versets.trad: Platters of gold and goblets shall be passed round among them; therein is all that souls desire and that delights the eyes. And there you shall abide forever.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī distinguishes two paradises according to the degrees: the worshippers have there what their souls desire, for they endured here below, through their spiritual struggles, hunger, thirst, and a thousand pains; they are rewarded in the Garden with every kind of recompense. But the people of gnosis and the lovers have what delights their eyes: the gaze upon God — so long was what they suffered of the excess of their longing and the consuming ardor of their desire. To some, the delights of the Garden; to others, the vision of the Face.

mots.racine: ص ح ف / ذ ه ب
mots.def: ṣaḥfa (pl. ṣiḥāf): dish, platter; dhahab: gold.

mots.racine: ش ه و / ن ف س
mots.def: ishtahā: to desire; nafs (pl. anfus): soul.

versets.ar: وَتِلْكَ الْجَنَّةُ الَّتِي أُورِثْتُمُوهَا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ
versets.tr: wa-tilka l-jannatu llatī ūrithtumūhā bimā kuntum taʿmalūna
versets.trad: Such is the Garden you are made to inherit for what you used to do.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī specifies to whom the word is addressed: it will be said to the obedient — you, people of sincerity (ikhlāṣ) in your works and of truthfulness (ṣidq) in your states. The Garden is an “inheritance”: not a wage wrested but a gift granted in regard for pure works. What turns the act into a title of inheritance is the sincerity of intention and the truthfulness of the inner state, not the mere quantity of works.

mots.sens: you are made to inherit it
mots.def: awratha: to cause to inherit; waritha: to inherit.

versets.ar: لَكُمْ فِيهَا فَاكِهَةٌ كَثِيرَةٌ مِّنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ
versets.tr: lakum fīhā fākihatun kathīratun minhā taʾkulūna
versets.trad: Therein for you is abundant fruit, of which you shall eat.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī adds a nuance of intimacy to the gift of fruits: of the abundant fruit you shall eat, and in intimacy (uns) you shall be received. The food of the Garden is not only material abundance but blessed companionship, welcome into the divine familiarity. The true delight of Paradise is not only to eat the fruits but to be received into the loving nearness of the Lord.

mots.racine: ف ك ه / ك ث ر

versets.ar: إِنَّ الْمُجْرِمِينَ فِي عَذَابِ جَهَنَّمَ خَالِدُونَ
versets.tr: inna l-mujrimīna fī ʿadhābi jahannama khālidūna
versets.trad: The guilty shall abide forever in the chastisement of Hell,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī distinguishes: these are the unbelieving associators, those who abide eternally; the chastisement neither slackens nor lightens for them. As for the people of Oneness, some may know the Fire, but they are not eternal in it. The eternity of the penalty is reserved for those who denied the Oneness; for the sinning believer, the trial has a term. The door of hope stays ajar for whoever has kept faith, however failing he may be.

versets.ar: لَا يُفَتَّرُ عَنْهُمْ وَهُمْ فِيهِ مُبْلِسُونَ
versets.tr: lā yufattaru ʿanhum wa-hum fīhi mublisūna
versets.trad: which will not be lightened for them, and therein they shall remain in despair.
versets.commentaire: From the word “mublisūn” (despairing) Qushayrī draws an inverse light: iblās is despair; this indicates that the believers, for their part, do not despair in the Fire — though they be in their trial, they remain in the state of their hope, counting their days until the term of their reckoning. And he cites this verse: “The flaw of safety is that its host dreads the blows of fate; the merit of trial is that its people watch — at the end of hope — for the affection of Time.”

mots.sens: it is not lightened for them

mots.def: ablasa: to despair, fall silent in despair.

versets.ar: وَمَا ظَلَمْنَاهُمْ وَلَٰكِن كَانُوا هُمُ الظَّالِمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-mā ẓalamnāhum wa-lākin kānū humu ẓ-ẓālimīna
versets.trad: We did not wrong them, but it was they who were the wrongdoers.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī finely observes that this saying resembles a word of excuse — though God, glory to Him, is far too exalted to need to excuse Himself. God deigns to justify His decree, not out of need but out of condescension toward the servants. The injustice comes not from Him but from themselves: they wronged themselves in choosing denial. The chastisement is but the exact return of their own act upon them.

mots.def: ẓalama: to be unjust, to wrong.

versets.ar: وَنَادَوْا يَا مَالِكُ لِيَقْضِ عَلَيْنَا رَبُّكَ ۖ قَالَ إِنَّكُم مَّاكِثُونَ
versets.tr: wa-nādaw yā māliku li-yaqḍi ʿalaynā rabbuka qāla innakum mākithūna
versets.trad: They will cry: “O Mālik! Let your Lord make an end of us!” He will say: “You are to remain!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī notes the subtlety of their error even in the invocation: had they said “O our Lord” instead of “O Mālik,” perhaps their words would have been closer to being answered; but the estrangement (ajnabiyya) stood between them and that. They address the keeper rather than the Lord: their prayer stays at the door. The allusion is that invocation must turn wholly toward the Lord alone; deflected to another, it remains without issue.

mots.def: Mālik: the angel keeper of Hell.

versets.ar: لَقَدْ جِئْنَاكُم بِالْحَقِّ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَكُمْ لِلْحَقِّ كَارِهُونَ
versets.tr: laqad jiʾnākum bi-l-ḥaqqi wa-lākinna aktharakum li-l-ḥaqqi kārihūna
versets.trad: We brought you the Truth, but most of you were averse to the Truth.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī links this verse to the sentence “you are to remain here”: you were counseled and did not accept the counsel, you did not receive the word in its time, and most of you hated the Truth. Aversion to the true is the root of misery: it is not that the Truth was lacking, but that the heart repelled it. The Truth was offered at the appointed time; to refuse it then is to condemn oneself.

mots.racine: ج ي أ / ح ق ق

versets.ar: أَمْ أَبْرَمُوا أَمْرًا فَإِنَّا مُبْرِمُونَ
versets.tr: am abramū amran fa-innā mubrimūna
versets.trad: Or have they devised some scheme? Then We too are devising!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: rather, their affairs come undone against them: nothing they have plotted succeeds, no design rises as they had reckoned — and this very failure is the most dazzling proof of the Creator's existence. The plot of men is overturned by a higher decree that dissolves it. That human contrivances always miscarry against their author reveals the hand of an Ordainer who alone holds the threads.

mots.racine: ب ر م / أ م ر
mots.def: abrama: to settle firmly, to plot.

versets.ar: أَمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّا لَا نَسْمَعُ سِرَّهُمْ وَنَجْوَاهُم ۚ بَلَىٰ وَرُسُلُنَا لَدَيْهِمْ يَكْتُبُونَ
versets.tr: am yaḥsabūna annā lā nasmaʿu sirrahum wa-najwāhum balā wa-rusulunā ladayhim yaktubūna
versets.trad: Or do they think We do not hear their secret and their private talk? Indeed We do! And Our messengers are with them, recording.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī draws a lesson from this warning: He frightened them with the King's hearing and with the recording of their deeds because they were heedless of God; for had they any good from God, He would not have frightened them by other than Him. Whoever knows that his deeds are written, and that he will be held responsible for them, reduces his dealings with what he would fear to be questioned about. To know one is heard and recorded is already to purify oneself.

mots.racine: س ر ر / ن ج و
mots.sens: their secret and their private talk
mots.def: sirr: inner secret; najwā: confidential talk.

mots.racine: ر س ل / ك ت ب
mots.def: rusul: the sent angels; kataba: to write.

versets.ar: قُلْ إِن كَانَ لِلرَّحْمَٰنِ وَلَدٌ فَأَنَا أَوَّلُ الْعَابِدِينَ
versets.tr: qul in kāna li-r-raḥmāni waladun fa-anā awwalu l-ʿābidīna
versets.trad: Say: “Had the Compassionate a son, I would be the first of worshippers!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reads the verse as a rhetorical challenge: if, in your thought, your judgment, and your belief, the Compassionate had a child, I would be the first to repudiate such a saying. The formula, far from conceding, denounces the absurdity: to posit the hypothesis the better to refute it. True worship is conceivable only of the One without associate or offspring; pure servanthood demands pure transcendence.

mots.racine: أ و ل / ع ب د

versets.ar: سُبْحَانَ رَبِّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ رَبِّ الْعَرْشِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ
versets.tr: subḥāna rabbi s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi rabbi l-ʿarshi ʿammā yaṣifūna
versets.trad: Glory to the Lord of the heavens and the earth, the Lord of the Throne, far above what they ascribe to Him!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: God declares Himself transcendent, infinitely holy above what they say. The tasbīḥ (glorification) is the response to blasphemy: one washes the Name soiled by praising Its purity. The Lord of the Throne, Master of heavens and earth, is beyond every attribute that the imagination of men ascribes to Him. To glorify is to restore to God His transcendence, which the false descriptions had veiled.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / ع ر ش

versets.ar: فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا وَيَلْعَبُوا حَتَّىٰ يُلَاقُوا يَوْمَهُمُ الَّذِي يُوعَدُونَ
versets.tr: fa-dharhum yakhūḍū wa-yalʿabū ḥattā yulāqū yawmahumu lladhī yūʿadūna
versets.trad: Leave them, then, to their idle talk and play until they meet the Day they are promised.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: for their affair will not escape them, and they will inevitably meet their humiliation. And he draws a warning for the believer: it does not befit the servant to be deceived by the length of his security, for the outcomes are not guaranteed. The respite of the deniers is no acquittal; and the believer's prolonged security must not lull him. The promised Day comes for all.

mots.racine: خ و ض / ل ع ب
mots.def: khāḍa: to plunge into the vain; laʿiba: to play.

mots.racine: ي و م / و ع د

versets.ar: وَهُوَ الَّذِي فِي السَّمَاءِ إِلَٰهٌ وَفِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَٰهٌ ۚ وَهُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْعَلِيمُ
versets.tr: wa-huwa lladhī fī s-samāʾi ilāhun wa-fī l-arḍi ilāhun wa-huwa l-ḥakīmu l-ʿalīmu
versets.trad: He it is who is God in heaven and God on earth. He is the Wise, the All-Knowing.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the Worshipped in heaven is God, and the One toward whom one turns for needs on earth is God. The dwellers of heaven worship none but God, and the dwellers of earth see their needs met by none but God. One God, above and below, undivided. And he glosses: “the Wise” by His forbearance toward the rebellious, “the All-Knowing” by His knowledge of the servants' states. The same Presence governs both worlds.

mots.racine: س م و / أ ل ه
ح ك م / ع ل م

versets.ar: وَتَبَارَكَ الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا وَعِندَهُ عِلْمُ السَّاعَةِ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ
versets.tr: wa-tabāraka lladhī lahu mulku s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi wa-mā baynahumā wa-ʿindahu ʿilmu s-sāʿati wa-ilayhi turjaʿūna
versets.trad: Blessed is He to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and all between them. With Him is the knowledge of the Hour, and to Him you shall be returned.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī meditates on the absolute sovereignty: the heavens and the earth appear by His power — it is not He who is exalted by their appearing. God is not made great by His creation; rather it exists only through Him. The Sufi rises from the created toward the Creator, present and contemplated, prior to every contingent. And he cites Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh: “When were you ever absent, that you should need a proof to lead you to Him?”

mots.def: tabāraka: to be exalted, abounding in blessing.

mots.racine: ع ل م / س و ع
mots.sens: with Him the knowledge of the Hour
mots.def: ʿilm as-sāʿa: knowledge of the final Hour.

versets.ar: وَلَا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ الشَّفَاعَةَ إِلَّا مَن شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lā yamliku lladhīna yadʿūna min dūnihi sh-shafāʿata illā man shahida bi-l-ḥaqqi wa-hum yaʿlamūna
versets.trad: Those they invoke besides Him have no power of intercession, save those who bear witness to the Truth and have knowledge.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: whoever bears witness — today — to the Oneness, to that one the Real will grant the right to intercede. In this verse is proof that the intercession of all Muslims will be accepted tomorrow. Intercession is not a power of the idols but a grace promised to whoever has confessed tawḥīd here below. The sincere witness to Oneness in this life opens the door of intercession in the next.

mots.racine: ش ه د / ح ق ق
mots.sens: who bears witness to the Truth
mots.def: shahida: to testify; ḥaqq: the Truth, tawḥīd.

versets.ar: وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّنْ خَلَقَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللَّهُ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ
versets.tr: wa-laʾin saʾaltahum man khalaqahum la-yaqūlunna llāhu fa-annā yuʾfakūna
versets.trad: If you ask them who created them, they will surely say: “God!” How then are they turned away?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī marvels with the verse: how do they not take the lesson? How do they grow proud to the point of refusing obedience to God? They confess that God created them, yet they turn from Him: such is the contradiction of the denying soul, which acknowledges the Origin and flees the Author. To acknowledge the Creator with the lips without obeying Him is the height of blindness.

mots.def: ufika: to be turned away, deluded.

versets.ar: وَقِيلِهِ يَا رَبِّ إِنَّ هَٰؤُلَاءِ قَوْمٌ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ
versets.tr: wa-qīlihi yā rabbi inna hāʾulāʾi qawmun lā yuʾminūna
versets.trad: And his cry: “O my Lord, these are a people who do not believe!”
versets.commentaire: The Prophet entrusts to God the grief of their unbelief: “O my Lord, these are a people who do not believe.” Qushayrī notes that God knows this plaint “O my Lord” as He knows the knowledge of the Hour. The servant who has exhausted the call turns to God and entrusts to Him the fate of the closed hearts; his very weariness becomes a prayer, and his sorrow rises to the Lord who hears it.

mots.sens: a people who do not believe

versets.ar: فَاصْفَحْ عَنْهُمْ وَقُلْ سَلَامٌ ۚ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ
versets.tr: fa-ṣfaḥ ʿanhum wa-qul salāmun fa-sawfa yaʿlamūna
versets.trad: So turn away from them and say: “Peace!” For they shall come to know.
versets.commentaire: The sūra seals itself on clemency. Qushayrī: “turn away from them” means: grant them a respite; “say: Peace” — from me upon you the salutation; but “they shall come to know” the recompense of what they deserve. The final “Peace” is not approval but greatness of soul: the Prophet quits the dispute by the salutation, leaving to God the care of judgment. To withdraw in peace, without renouncing the truth, is the summit of prophetic patience.

mots.def: ṣafaḥa: to forgive, turn away with indulgence.
